content:
# Genesis Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2r–2v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_002_page_001.jpg, GEN_002_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And the heavens and the earth were completed, and all their host.

**2** And God completed on the seventh day His work which He had done, and He rested on the seventh day from all His work which He had done.

**3** And God blessed the seventh day and sanctified it, for on it He rested from all His work which God had created to make.

**4** These are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made earth and heavens.

**5** And no shrub of the field was yet on the earth, and no plant of the field had yet sprung up, for the LORD God had not caused rain upon the earth, and there was no man to work the ground.

**6** And a mist went up from the earth and watered the whole face of the ground.

**7** And the LORD God formed the man of dust from the ground, and He breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living soul.

**8** And the LORD God planted a garden in Eden, to the east, and He placed there the man whom He had formed.

**9** And the LORD God caused to sprout from the ground every tree that is pleasant to the sight and good for food, and the tree of life in the midst of the garden, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.

**10** And a river was going out from Eden to water the garden, and from there it divided and became four heads.

**11** The name of the first is Pishon; it is the one surrounding the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.

**12** And the gold of that land is good; the bdellium and the onyx stone are there.

**13** And the name of the second river is Gihon; it is the one surrounding the whole land of Cush.

**14** And the name of the third river is Tigris; it is the one going to the east of Asshur. And the fourth river — it is the Euphrates.

**15** And the LORD God took the man and placed him in the garden of Eden, to work it and to keep it.

**16** And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, "From every tree of the garden you may freely eat.

**17** But from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil — you shall not eat from it, for in the day of your eating from it, you shall surely die."

**18** And the LORD God said, "It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make for him a helper corresponding to him."

**19** And the LORD God formed from the ground every beast of the field and every bird of the heavens, and He brought each to the man to see what he would call it. And whatever the man called each living creature — that was its name.

**20** And the man gave names to all the livestock, and to the birds of the heavens, and to every beast of the field. But for the man, there was not found a helper corresponding to him.

**21** And the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man, and he slept. And He took one of his ribs, and He closed up the flesh in its place.

**22** And the LORD God built the rib which He had taken from the man into a woman, and He brought her to the man.

**23** And the man said, "This one, this time — bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh. This one shall be called Woman, for from Man was this one taken."

**24** Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.

**25** And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and they were not ashamed.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscript
This translation continues from Genesis 1, produced from direct reading of the Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A), dated c. 1008 CE. The photographs are from the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 2 spans Folios 2r–2v, beginning in the rightmost column of 2r (following Gen 1:31) and continuing through 2v. The three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation is maintained throughout.

### Structural Note: The Parashah Division
The Masoretic text marks a setumah (closed paragraph) or petuchah (open paragraph) break between 2:3 and 2:4. This is one of the most significant textual boundaries in Genesis: it separates the creation liturgy of 1:1–2:3 from the Eden narrative of 2:4–25. The phrase אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת (elleh toledot, "these are the generations") in 2:4 introduces the first of the toledot formulae that structure the entire book of Genesis.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **וַיְכֻלּוּ (vayekhullu) — "were completed"** (v. 1): From the root כלה, "to be complete, to be finished." The Pual form is passive: the heavens and earth were completed — not merely "finished" in a casual sense, but brought to their fullness. The subject צְבָאָם (tseva'am, "their host") encompasses the totality of created beings that populate heavens and earth.

2. **וַיְכַל אֱלֹהִים בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי (vayekhal elohim bayyom hashevi'i) — "And God completed on the seventh day"**: The Leningrad Codex, along with the Masoretic Text, reads "seventh day" here, not "sixth day" as in the Samaritan Pentateuch, LXX, and Peshitta. Those versions emend to avoid the implication that God worked on the seventh day. The Masoretic reading is the more difficult (lectio difficilior) and is preserved: God's final act of completion belongs to the seventh day; the rest then follows.

3. **וַיִּשְׁבֹּת (vayyishbot) — "He rested"**: From the root שׁבת, the verbal form behind the noun שַׁבָּת (Shabbat). "Rested" is retained rather than "ceased," though the Hebrew carries both senses — cessation from labor and the positive state of rest. The threefold repetition of "the seventh day" in vv. 2–3 is architectonic and liturgical; it is preserved without variation.

4. **יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (YHWH elohim) — "the LORD God"**: Beginning in 2:4, the divine name shifts from אֱלֹהִים alone (used throughout Gen 1:1–2:3) to the compound יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים. Following longstanding convention, the Tetragrammaton (יהוה) is rendered "LORD" in small capitals conceptually (here in all caps for markdown), preserving the distinction from אֱלֹהִים ("God"). The compound name appears throughout the Eden narrative (2:4–3:24) and is unique to this section of Genesis. It binds the cosmic Creator (Elohim) to the covenant God of Israel (YHWH).

5. **תּוֹלְדוֹת (toledot) — "generations"** (v. 4): Literally "begettings," from the root ילד, "to bear, to beget." This is the first of ten (or eleven) toledot formulae that structure Genesis (cf. 5:1, 6:9, 10:1, 11:10, 11:27, 25:12, 25:19, 36:1, 36:9, 37:2). "Generations" is retained as the standard formal rendering, carrying both the sense of genealogical descent and narrative unfolding.

6. **אָדָם / אֲדָמָה (adam / adamah) — "man" / "ground"**: The wordplay between אָדָם (adam, "man") and אֲדָמָה (adamah, "ground") is central to 2:7 and pervades the chapter. The man is formed from the ground; he is the earth-creature, the groundling. English cannot replicate the phonological link, but "man" and "ground" are consistently used to preserve the conceptual pairing. A note is warranted: the Hebrew reader hears in every mention of "the man" an echo of the soil from which he was taken.

7. **נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים (nishmat chayyim) — "the breath of life"** (v. 7): Distinguished from רוּחַ (ruach, "spirit/wind") and נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, "soul/being"). The נְשָׁמָה (neshamah) is specifically the breath blown into the nostrils — intimate, face-to-face divine animation. The result: the man "became a living soul" (לְנֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה, lenefesh chayyah), the same phrase applied to animals in 1:20, 24. What distinguishes the man is not a different category of soul but the directness of divine respiration.

8. **אֵד (ed) — "mist"** (v. 6): This rare word (occurring only here and Job 36:27) is of uncertain meaning. "Mist" follows the traditional rendering. Other proposals include "spring," "stream," or "flood" (cf. Akkadian edû, "flood"). The Targumim render it as "cloud." I have retained "mist" as a conservative choice that does not over-specify, while noting the genuine lexical uncertainty.

9. **עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (ezer kenegdo) — "a helper corresponding to him"** (vv. 18, 20): This phrase is notoriously difficult to render. עֵזֶר (ezer) means "help, helper" and is used elsewhere of God Himself as Israel's help (Ps 33:20, 70:6, 121:1–2). It carries no connotation of subordination. כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (kenegdo) means "as opposite to him, as corresponding to him, as his counterpart." I render the full phrase "a helper corresponding to him" to convey both the relational complementarity and the equality of nature. "Helpmate" and "help meet" are avoided as they have accrued misleading connotations.

10. **צֵלָע (tsela) — "rib"** (vv. 21–22): The word more broadly means "side" (it is used architecturally of the side-chambers of the temple in 1 Kgs 6:5, and of the side of the Ark in Exod 25:12). "Rib" is the traditional rendering and is retained, but the reader should note that "side" is within the semantic range — the woman is built from the man's very side, not merely a single bone.

11. **וַיִּבֶן (vayyiben) — "built"** (v. 22): The LORD God "built" (בנה, banah) the rib into a woman. The verb is architectural, not biological — the same root used for building altars, cities, and temples. This is deliberate and striking: the woman is not merely "made" (עשׂה) or "formed" (יצר) but constructed with the intentionality of a builder. The verb is preserved literally.

12. **אִשָּׁה / אִישׁ (ishah / ish) — "Woman" / "Man"** (v. 23): The man's exclamation plays on the phonological similarity of אִישׁ (ish, "man") and אִשָּׁה (ishah, "woman"). English "man/woman" partially preserves this. The Hebrew is not strictly etymological (ishah does not actually derive from ish), but the folk-etymological wordplay is the narrator's point. "This one shall be called Woman, for from Man was this one taken" preserves the explanatory structure.

13. **זֹאת הַפַּעַם (zot happa'am) — "This one, this time"** (v. 23): The man's first recorded speech. The demonstrative זֹאת (zot, "this one") combined with הַפַּעַם (happa'am, "this time / this instance") expresses recognition and delight — after naming the animals and finding none that corresponded to him, at last here is one who does. "This one, this time" captures both the emphatic demonstrative and the temporal sense of arrival.

14. **וְדָבַק (vedavaq) — "shall cleave"** (v. 24): From דבק, "to cling, to adhere, to join." The word is used elsewhere of covenantal loyalty (Deut 10:20, "to the LORD your God you shall cleave"; Ruth 1:14). "Cleave" is retained for its archaic dignity and its theological resonance; "cling" or "be joined" would also be defensible, but "cleave" preserves the register of the text.

15. **עֲרוּמִּים (arummim) — "naked"** (v. 25): The chapter closes with the man and woman "naked" (עֲרוּמִּים) and "not ashamed" (וְלֹא יִתְבֹּשָׁשׁוּ). The word עָרוּם will appear immediately in 3:1 to describe the serpent as "crafty" — a different word (עָרוּם, "shrewd, cunning") but phonologically identical. This wordplay bridges the innocence of chapter 2 and the cunning of chapter 3. The English reader should be alert to this: "naked" and "crafty" sound the same in Hebrew.

16. **Consistency with Genesis 1**: "God" (elohim), "expanse" (raqia, which does not appear in chapter 2), "living soul" / "living creature" (nefesh chayyah) — all terms are rendered consistently with the Genesis 1 translation. The shift to "the LORD God" (YHWH Elohim) in 2:4 is the only change in divine naming, and it is dictated by the Hebrew text itself.

— LOGOS

---

## Supplementary Notes — Additional Manuscripts
**Review date:** 2026-03-04 | **Reviewer:** LOGOS
**Additional sources examined:**
- Dead Sea Scrolls: 4QGen-a, 4QGen-b
- Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE
- **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209
- **View DSS online:** https://www.deadseascrolls.org.il/

---

### I. Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QGen-a and 4QGen-b

The relevant Qumran fragments, designated 4QGen-a and 4QGen-b, preserve portions of Genesis 2 in Hebrew. Their overall testimony for this chapter is conservative: they show no major departures from the Masoretic tradition as represented by the Leningrad Codex, and no DSS reading requires a revision to any translation decision made above.

**Orthographic variants only.** The fragments exhibit the expected Qumran scribal conventions: plene (full) spelling where the MT vocalizes with assumed matres lectionis, occasional interchange of waw and yod in medial positions, and minor variation in particle attachment (waw-conjunctive prefixing). None of these affect the consonantal base of any word whose translation is in dispute.

**Confirmation of MT consonantal text.** Where legible, 4QGen-a and 4QGen-b confirm the consonantal skeleton of the MT at every point that has a bearing on this translation. This includes the form of the divine names, the root יצר in v. 7, the term עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ in v. 18, the root דבק in v. 24, and the root בנה in v. 22. The DSS do not resolve the 'ed / πηγή question (v. 6) since the Hebrew consonants are identical whichever lexical tradition one follows; they simply read אֵד as in the MT.

**Conclusion for the translation:** The DSS provide independent, earlier attestation of the Hebrew text that underlies this translation. They do not destabilize any existing rendering. Their principal value for this chapter is corroborative: they confirm that the MT represents an ancient, well-preserved textual tradition rather than a late medieval peculiarity.

---

### II. Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX Readings for Genesis 2

The two pages of Vaticanus containing Genesis 2 have been examined directly. The manuscript (folio 3 and folio 4 in the digital edition, at Vat.gr.1209/0007 and /0008) preserves the text in an uncial hand characteristic of the mid-4th century, written in three columns per page with no verse divisions. The Greek runs continuously, with majuscule letters, minimal punctuation, and no word spacing — the standard scriptio continua format of this period. Both pages are fully legible, and the relevant variant readings can be confirmed visually in the manuscript.

The following notes address each LXX variant in turn, assess its textual significance, and state its effect — or non-effect — on this translation.

---

**2:2 — τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ ("on the sixth day") vs. MT "on the seventh day"**

Vaticanus reads τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ ("on the sixth day"), agreeing with the Samaritan Pentateuch and the Syriac Peshitta against the MT's "seventh day." The LXX translator — or the Vorlage behind it — appears to have harmonized 2:2 to avoid the reading that God was still "completing" His work on the seventh day (which would imply labor on the Sabbath). The Samaritan Pentateuch's independent agreement with the LXX reading is noteworthy: it suggests this was a widespread scribal concern, not a uniquely Greek emendation.

However, the MT "seventh day" is retained in this translation, for reasons already stated in note 2 above: it is the lectio difficilior. The more theologically difficult reading — the one that creates an apparent contradiction, that God completed work on the day He then blessed as a day of rest — is the one scribes would have been motivated to smooth away. The LXX and SP reflect that smoothing. The MT preserves the harder and therefore more original reading. Vaticanus confirms the LXX tradition faithfully, but the argument for the MT's priority is not weakened by that confirmation.

**Translation stands: "on the seventh day."**

---

**2:4 — αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως ("this is the book of generation") vs. MT אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת ("these are the generations")**

Vaticanus reads αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς ("this is the book of generation of heaven and earth"). The LXX inserts βίβλος ("book") into the toledot formula, and it uses the singular γενέσεως ("of generation," genitive singular) where the MT has the plural תוֹלְדוֹת. Both differences are significant.

The addition of βίβλος is interpretive: the LXX translator understood the toledot heading as a reference to a written document — "this is the book of the genesis of heaven and earth." This is a coherent ancient reading, and it gave the LXX its title for the whole book: ΓΕΝΕΣΙΣ, Genesis. The word βίβλος does not appear in the Hebrew; the MT has no word corresponding to "book" here. Its insertion in the LXX reveals the translator's sense that the toledot formulae function as literary headings — section-markers in a textual collection.

Nevertheless, this translation continues to render אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת as "These are the generations," following the MT. The LXX addition cannot be retroversed into the Hebrew with any confidence; βίβλος is the translator's gloss, not the text. The value of the LXX reading here is hermeneutical rather than textual: it attests an early Jewish interpretive tradition that understood the toledot formula as bookish and archival. That tradition informs the reading notes without changing the translation.

Note also: the LXX use of γενέσεως (singular) rather than a plural corresponding to the MT plural תוֹלְדוֹת is a further instance of the Greek translator's tendency to resolve the syntactic ambiguity of the formula into a cleaner Greek construction.

**Translation stands: "These are the generations."**

---

**2:6 — πηγή ("spring / fountain") vs. MT אֵד ("mist")**

This is the most consequential LXX variant in Genesis 2 for this translation, and it requires the most careful treatment.

Vaticanus reads πηγὴ δὲ ἀνέβαινεν ἐκ τῆς γῆς ("but a spring was coming up from the earth"), rendering the Hebrew אֵד with πηγή ("spring, fountain, source"), not with any word for "mist," "vapor," or "cloud."

The existing note (note 8) already documented the lexical uncertainty of אֵד: the word appears only twice in the Hebrew Bible (here and Job 36:27), its etymology is disputed, and ancient interpreters were divided — the Targumim rendered it as "cloud," Aquila and Symmachus appear to have known a "mist" or "vapor" tradition, and the LXX now weighs in firmly for "spring" or "fountain."

The LXX reading πηγή is almost certainly not a guess. The Greek translator working from a Hebrew Vorlage read אֵד as belonging to the semantic field of subterranean water — a spring or flood-source welling up from the earth. This is consistent with the Akkadian cognate edû, which Moran and others have associated with the primordial flood-waters of the Mesopotamian tradition. If the LXX translator's Vorlage read or understood אֵד as a spring or underground source, then v. 6 would describe a subterranean irrigation: water rising up from beneath the earth to water the surface, as opposed to rain coming down from above (which did not yet exist, per v. 5). This makes excellent geological and narrative sense in the ancient Near Eastern context: the earth is watered from below, by the tehom-like underground waters, before the garden is planted and before the rain-cycle is established.

The interpretive significance is real. "Mist" (as in my current rendering) suggests a surface phenomenon — atmospheric vapor. "Spring" or "fountain" (as in the LXX) suggests a subterranean source, a well-spring. These imply different cosmological pictures.

**Effect on translation choice:** After weighing the LXX evidence, I maintain "mist" in the translation text, but with revised confidence. My earlier note described "mist" as a conservative choice that "does not over-specify." The LXX evidence reinforces that honest caution: it confirms that ancient translators working directly from the Hebrew did not agree that the word meant "mist." The LXX's πηγή represents an ancient, linguistically serious alternative. A footnote to the translation text would now read: *"Or 'spring, fountain' (LXX πηγή; cf. Akkadian edû). The word occurs only here and Job 36:27; its precise meaning is disputed among ancient and modern interpreters alike."*

**Translation stands: "mist" — but this is acknowledged as a provisional rendering, with the LXX "spring" preserved as a serious textual alternative in the notes.**

---

**2:7 — ἔπλασεν ("molded / formed") for יָצַר (yatsar)**

Vaticanus reads καὶ ἔπλασεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν ἄνθρωπον ("and God molded the man"), using ἔπλασσεν / ἔπλασεν — the verb πλάσσω, which is the standard Greek term for a potter shaping clay. This is the same verb used of potters at work in Greek literature and of God as divine craftsman in later Christian and Jewish texts. The LXX here makes explicit what is implicit in the Hebrew: yatsar is the potter's verb, and ἔπλασεν is the potter's verb in Greek.

This confirms the translation note on yatsar (note in the Disagreements Log, and in note 6 above implicitly through the adam/adamah wordplay). The man is clay in the hands of the divine Potter. Both the Hebrew and the Greek agree on this image with precision.

The LXX does not preserve the adam/adamah wordplay (ἄνθρωπος, "man/human," does not echo χοῦς, "dust," or γῆ, "earth," in the same way אָדָם echoes אֲדָמָה). This is a Greek limitation, not a textual disagreement. The potter imagery, however, is fully preserved and confirmed.

**Translation stands: "formed." The LXX confirms the potter's image that underlies the Hebrew verb.**

---

**2:7 — ψυχὴν ζῶσαν ("living soul") for נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה (nefesh chayyah)**

Vaticanus reads καὶ ἐγένετο ὁ ἄνθρωπος εἰς ψυχὴν ζῶσαν ("and the man became a living soul"). The LXX renders נֶפֶשׁ with ψυχή throughout the Pentateuch, and ψυχὴν ζῶσαν here is a direct, unambiguous confirmation of the rendering "living soul." This is also the LXX phrase that the Apostle Paul cites in 1 Corinthians 15:45, contrasting ψυχὴν ζῶσαν with πνεῦμα ζῳοποιοῦν ("life-giving spirit") — a distinction that depends on the Greek LXX text.

The consensus rendering "living soul" (adopted over PNEUMA's "living being" in the Disagreements Log) is thus confirmed by two independent ancient sources: the MT's נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה and the LXX's ψυχὴν ζῶσαν. The LXX's independent testimony settles the question of whether the Hebrew was understood in antiquity as referring to an animated, ensouled being rather than a merely biological entity.

**Translation stands and is confirmed: "living soul."**

---

**2:8 — παράδεισον ("paradise") for גַּן (gan, "garden")**

Vaticanus reads καὶ ἐφύτευσεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς παράδεισον ἐν Εδεμ ("and the LORD God planted a paradise in Eden"), using παράδεισος (paradeisos) for the Hebrew גַּן (gan).

Παράδεισος is a Greek transliteration of the Old Persian *pairi-daeza* (Avestan *pairi-daēza*), meaning an enclosed royal park or walled garden — the pleasure gardens of Persian kings. The word entered Greek through Xenophon's Anabasis and related literature describing the Persian Empire's royal estates. When the LXX translators chose παράδεισος for גַּן, they were reaching for the most magnificent enclosure their cultural vocabulary could supply: not merely a kitchen garden but a royal, enclosed, exquisitely cultivated domain.

This has significant translation implications. My rendering "garden" follows the Hebrew גַּן and is appropriate for a formal equivalence approach: "garden" is what the MT says, and it is the correct lexical rendering. But the LXX's choice of παράδεισος reveals how ancient Jewish translators understood the theological weight of the word: the Garden of Eden is not an ordinary plot of ground. It is the garden of the cosmic King, an enclosed paradise, the prototypical Paradise. The English word "paradise" descends directly from this Greek through Latin *paradisus*, and it arrived in the English Bible via precisely this verse. To translate gan as "garden" is accurate; to note that the LXX says "paradise" illuminates the full theological tradition.

This note does not affect the translation text — "garden" is retained as the formal rendering of גַּן — but it establishes that the word carries royal, sacred, and cosmic weight that the English "garden" alone does not convey.

**Translation stands: "garden." The LXX παράδεισος is noted as the source of the English theological term "paradise."**

---

**2:18 — βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν ("helper according to him / corresponding to him") for עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (ezer kenegdo)**

Vaticanus reads ποιήσωμεν αὐτῷ βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν ("let us make for him a helper according to him / corresponding to him"). The LXX renders:

- עֵזֶר (ezer) → βοηθόν (boethon, accusative of βοηθός, "helper, one who comes to aid")
- כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (kenegdo) → κατ᾽ αὐτόν ("according to him / after him / in correspondence with him")

This is a direct confirmation of the rendering adopted in this translation: "a helper corresponding to him." The Greek κατ᾽ αὐτόν uses the preposition κατά with the accusative pronoun αὐτόν, conveying the sense of correspondence, equivalence, or facing — "according to him, in proportion to him, facing him." This is precisely what kenegdo expresses in Hebrew: the woman is the man's counterpart, his facing-one, his correspond-ent.

Βοηθός is the LXX's standard rendering for עֵזֶר throughout the Hebrew Bible, including in the Psalms where ezer describes God as Israel's helper. Its use here confirms that ezer carries no connotation of subordination: βοηθός is used of God the helper, of armies coming to the aid of besieged cities, of one person coming to the rescue of another. It is a word of strength and active assistance, not servitude.

The Greek βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν maps with remarkable precision onto my rendering "a helper corresponding to him." The LXX translator made the same two moves I made: preserve "helper" as the primary relational category, and render kenegdo with a term of correspondence or proportionality. That a 3rd–2nd century BCE Jewish translator working directly from the Hebrew arrived at the same structure independently — and that this rendering was then preserved in the great 4th century CE Vaticanus uncial — provides strong confirmation that this rendering faithfully represents the ancient understanding of the phrase.

**Translation stands and is confirmed: "a helper corresponding to him."**

---

**2:21 — ἔκστασιν ("ecstasy / trance") for תַּרְדֵּמָה (tardemah, "deep sleep")**

Vaticanus reads καὶ ἐπέβαλεν ὁ θεὸς ἔκστασιν ἐπὶ τὸν Αδαμ ("and God cast an ecstasy upon Adam"), using ἔκστασις (ekstasis) where the MT has תַּרְדֵּמָה (tardemah).

Tardemah is a heavy, supernaturally induced sleep — the same word used of Abram in Genesis 15:12 when God passes between the covenant animals. It is deep, overwhelming, and specifically divine in origin. My translation "a deep sleep" follows the Hebrew directly and is a faithful formal rendering.

The LXX's choice of ἔκστασις is interpretively richer and more theologically charged than "deep sleep." Ἔκστασις in classical and Hellenistic Greek means a displacement of the mind from its normal state — literally a "standing-outside-oneself." It can denote amazement, terror, or a visionary trance state. The word is used in the LXX elsewhere of prophetic states (e.g., the same word appears in the LXX of Gen 15:12 for Abram's tardemah). By rendering tardemah as ekstasis, the LXX translator understood the sleep of the man in v. 21 not merely as a medical anesthetic (so that he would not feel the operation) but as a visionary or ecstatic state — the man is not simply unconscious; he is in a God-induced trance, outside himself, while the woman is formed.

This is a significant interpretive enrichment. It would mean the man's sleep is structurally similar to prophetic vision: God acts while the human is in an altered, divinely-induced state of receptivity. The woman's formation, in this reading, is not merely a surgical procedure performed on an unconscious patient — it is a divine act occurring in a sacred, ecstatic space.

**Effect on translation:** "A deep sleep" is retained, as it most directly renders tardemah. However, the LXX ἔκστασις is noted as a serious ancient alternative that enriches the theological reading: *"LXX renders tardemah as ἔκστασιν (ekstasis), 'ecstasy / trance,' suggesting a divinely-induced visionary state rather than merely unconsciousness. Cf. Gen 15:12 LXX."*

**Translation stands: "a deep sleep." LXX ἔκστασιν is preserved in the notes as a significant interpretive tradition.**

---

**2:22 — ᾠκοδόμησεν ("built") for וַיִּבֶן (vayyiben, root בנה, banah)**

Vaticanus reads καὶ ᾠκοδόμησεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὴν πλευρὰν... εἰς γυναῖκα ("and the LORD God built the rib... into a woman"), using ᾠκοδόμησεν — the aorist of οἰκοδομέω, "to build, to construct, to erect a building." This is the standard Greek architectural verb, used throughout the LXX for building temples, houses, cities, and walls.

This is an unambiguous confirmation of the translation decision in note 11 above: "And the LORD God built the rib which He had taken from the man into a woman." Both the Hebrew בנה and the Greek οἰκοδομέω are architectural, constructive verbs. The LXX translator chose exactly the word that best mirrors the Hebrew: the same verb used of Solomon building the Temple (1 Kgs 6 LXX) is used here of God building the woman. The deliberateness and theological weight of the verb is thus confirmed across both language traditions.

**Translation stands and is confirmed: "built."**

---

**2:24 — προσκολληθήσεται ("shall be glued to / shall cleave to") for וְדָבַק (vedavaq)**

Vaticanus reads καὶ προσκολληθήσεται τῇ γυναικὶ αὐτοῦ ("and he shall be glued to his wife"), using the passive of προσκολλάω — literally "to glue to, to stick fast to, to adhere." The word προσκολλάω uses the preposition πρός ("toward") combined with κολλάω ("to glue"), from κόλλα ("glue"). It is a strong, physical, adhesive term.

The existing note (note 14) retains "shall cleave" on the grounds of covenantal dignity and theological resonance. The LXX confirms the physical intensity of the Hebrew דבק while adding a vivid adhesive image: this is not merely joining but being glued, cemented, permanently adhered. The word "cleave" in archaic English actually captures this well — the older meaning of "cleave" is "to stick, to adhere" (as in "cleave to"), distinct from the modern "cleave" meaning "to split." The LXX's προσκολλάω confirms that the ancient translators read דבק as an act of permanent, physical-spiritual adhesion, not a casual or separable attachment.

The note in the Disagreements Log confirmed "shall cleave" on grounds of covenantal register. Vaticanus now adds textual confirmation: the Greek preserves the same intensity, and "cleave" remains the English word best suited to carry both the adhesive force (προσκολλάω / דבק) and the covenantal register of the verse.

**Translation stands and is confirmed: "shall cleave."**

---

### III. Summary of Manuscript Evidence

| Verse | MT | LXX (Vaticanus) | DSS | Translation | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2:2 | "seventh day" | "sixth day" (τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ) | Confirms MT | "seventh day" | MT retained as lectio difficilior |
| 2:4 | toledot (no "book") | βίβλος γενέσεως ("book of generation") | Confirms MT | "These are the generations" | LXX insertion noted; MT followed |
| 2:6 | אֵד ("mist"?) | πηγή ("spring / fountain") | Confirms MT consonants | "mist" | Retained provisionally; LXX alternative noted |
| 2:7 | יצר / נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה | ἔπλασεν / ψυχὴν ζῶσαν | Confirms MT | "formed" / "living soul" | Both confirmed by LXX |
| 2:8 | גַּן ("garden") | παράδεισον ("paradise") | Confirms MT | "garden" | LXX theological tradition noted |
| 2:18 | עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ | βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν | Confirms MT | "a helper corresponding to him" | LXX confirms rendering with precision |
| 2:21 | תַּרְדֵּמָה ("deep sleep") | ἔκστασιν ("ecstasy / trance") | Confirms MT | "a deep sleep" | LXX visionary reading noted |
| 2:22 | בנה ("built") | ᾠκοδόμησεν ("built") | Confirms MT | "built" | LXX confirms architectural verb |
| 2:24 | דבק ("cleave / cling") | προσκολληθήσεται ("shall be glued") | Confirms MT | "shall cleave" | LXX confirms adhesive intensity |

**Overall assessment:** The DSS and Vaticanus do not require any change to the translation text of Genesis 2. They confirm the principal rendering decisions at every tested verse. The LXX does supply two readings that enrich the notes beyond the MT alone: the πηγή variant at v. 6 (which confirms that "mist" is a provisional rendering of a genuinely uncertain word) and the ἔκστασιν variant at v. 21 (which reveals an ancient tradition of reading the man's sleep as a visionary, prophetic state). Both of these are preserved in the notes for the reader's benefit, though neither displaces the MT-based translation text.

The textual tradition of Genesis 2 is robust and multi-attested. The MT, LXX, and DSS converge at every point that matters for this translation — with the understood and documented exception of the "sixth/seventh day" variant at v. 2, where the MT is retained on text-critical grounds.

— LOGOS
